summary: Any call row can create a new unordered pair or change an existing pair's count and duration, so exact aggregation has a linear inspection lower bound; one canonicalizing hash scan matches it.

optimality.argument: Changing any uninspected row can introduce a new participant pair or alter both aggregates for an existing pair, so every one of the R rows must be considered. Computing min/max-oriented IDs and updating a hash group takes expected O(1) work per row and O(R) total time.

replacement_checks.evidence: An adversary may place the only call for a new pair or an additional duration contribution in any uninspected row.
One hash-aggregation scan computes a constant-size canonical pair and updates COUNT and SUM once for each input row, with no required sort.
Authored fixtures cover the public sample, opposite directions, exact duplicates, reverse-only input, a single call, several pairs sharing a person, and unsorted pair IDs.
